answer:
URL Shortener with Analytics Dashboard

Core Definition

A URL shortener maps short codes to long URLs and redirects users. An analytics dashboard tracks click counts, referrer sources, geographic data, and time-series click data for each shortened URL.

Manoj's Project Context

Manoj would build this using his stack: Fastify for the redirect API (needs to be fast), MongoDB for storing URL mappings and click events, and React for the analytics dashboard with charts showing click trends.

Architecture Diagram

Code Example

// models: urls collection + clicks collection
const urlSchema = {
  shortCode: { type: String, unique: true, index: true },
  originalUrl: String,
  createdBy: String,
  createdAt: Date,
  clickCount: { type: Number, default: 0 },
};

const clickSchema = {
  shortCode: String,
  timestamp: Date,
  referrer: String,
  userAgent: String,
  ip: String,
};

// POST /api/shorten — Create short URL
app.post('/api/shorten', async (req, res) => {
  const { url } = req.body;
  const shortCode = nanoid(7); // e.g., "aB3x7Kp"
  await db.urls.insertOne({ shortCode, originalUrl: url, createdAt: new Date(), clickCount: 0 });
  res.json({ shortUrl: `${BASE_URL}/${shortCode}` });
});

// GET /:code — Redirect + track click
app.get('/:code', async (req, res) => {
  const url = await db.urls.findOne({ shortCode: req.params.code });
  if (!url) return res.status(404).send('Not found');

  // Track click asynchronously (don't block redirect)
  db.clicks.insertOne({
    shortCode: req.params.code,
    timestamp: new Date(),
    referrer: req.headers.referer || 'direct',
    userAgent: req.headers['user-agent'],
  });
  db.urls.updateOne({ shortCode: req.params.code }, { $inc: { clickCount: 1 } });

  res.redirect(301, url.originalUrl);
});

// GET /api/analytics/:code — Dashboard data
app.get('/api/analytics/:code', async (req, res) => {
  const [url, clicksByDay, topReferrers] = await Promise.all([
    db.urls.findOne({ shortCode: req.params.code }),
    db.clicks.aggregate([
      { $match: { shortCode: req.params.code } },
      { $group: { _id: { $dateToString: { format: '%Y-%m-%d', date: '$timestamp' } }, count: { $sum: 1 } } },
      { $sort: { _id: 1 } },
      { $limit: 30 },
    ]).toArray(),
    db.clicks.aggregate([
      { $match: { shortCode: req.params.code } },
      { $group: { _id: '$referrer', count: { $sum: 1 } } },
      { $sort: { count: -1 } },
      { $limit: 10 },
    ]).toArray(),
  ]);
  res.json({ url, clicksByDay, topReferrers });
});

Manoj's Debugging Narrative

Key design decisions: (1) Use `nanoid(7)` for short codes — 7 chars gives 3.5 trillion combinations. (2) Track clicks asynchronously — don't await the insert before redirecting, keeping redirect latency under 50ms. (3) Aggregate analytics with MongoDB's aggregation pipeline for time-series and top-referrer queries. (4) Index `shortCode` for O(1) lookups. The analytics dashboard uses Chart.js to display clicks-per-day line charts and referrer pie charts.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Short code collisionsUse nanoid with sufficient length (7+); retry on duplicate key error
Click tracking slows redirectFire-and-forget insert; don't await before redirecting
Analytics queries slow on large datasetsCreate indexes on shortCode + timestamp; use aggregation pipeline

How to Validate

  • Create short URL and visit it — verify redirect and click count increments
  • Check analytics endpoint — verify clicks-per-day and referrer data
  • Load test redirect endpoint — verify <50ms response time

Interview Scheduling Service with Conflict Detection

Core Definition

An interview scheduling service manages interview time slots, detects conflicts with existing events, and handles timezone-aware scheduling. Conflict detection ensures no interviewer or candidate is double-booked.

Manoj's Project Context

Building on his HR Leave Management System experience, Manoj would create this with Fastify + PostgreSQL/Prisma for transactional conflict detection, ensuring atomic scheduling even under concurrent requests.

Code Example

// Prisma schema
model Interview {
  id            String   @id @default(uuid())
  candidateId   String
  interviewerId String
  startTime     DateTime
  endTime       DateTime
  status        String   @default("scheduled") // scheduled, completed, cancelled
  @@index([interviewerId, startTime, endTime])
  @@index([candidateId, startTime, endTime])
}

// Conflict detection — check both interviewer and candidate availability
async function detectConflicts(data: {
  candidateId: string;
  interviewerId: string;
  startTime: Date;
  endTime: Date;
}): Promise<{ hasConflict: boolean; conflicts: any[] }> {
  const conflicts = await prisma.interview.findMany({
    where: {
      status: 'scheduled',
      OR: [
        { interviewerId: data.interviewerId },
        { candidateId: data.candidateId },
      ],
      // Overlapping time range check
      startTime: { lt: data.endTime },
      endTime: { gt: data.startTime },
    },
  });
  return { hasConflict: conflicts.length > 0, conflicts };
}

// POST /api/interviews — Schedule with conflict check
app.post('/api/interviews', async (req, res) => {
  const { candidateId, interviewerId, startTime, endTime } = req.body;

  // Use transaction for atomic conflict check + insert
  const result = await prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => {
    const { hasConflict, conflicts } = await detectConflicts({
      candidateId, interviewerId,
      startTime: new Date(startTime),
      endTime: new Date(endTime),
    });

    if (hasConflict) {
      return { error: 'Time conflict detected', conflicts };
    }

    return tx.interview.create({
      data: {
        candidateId, interviewerId,
        startTime: new Date(startTime),
        endTime: new Date(endTime),
      },
    });
  });

  if ('error' in result) return res.status(409).json(result);
  res.status(201).json(result);
});

Manoj's Debugging Narrative

Key patterns: (1) Time overlap detection uses `startTime < endTime AND endTime > startTime` — the standard interval overlap check. (2) Prisma transaction ensures the conflict check and insert happen atomically — prevents race conditions where two requests book the same slot simultaneously. (3) Composite indexes on (interviewerId, startTime, endTime) make conflict queries fast. (4) All times stored as UTC in PostgreSQL; frontend converts to user's timezone for display.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Race condition: two requests book same slotUse database transaction for atomic check-and-insert
Timezone confusion in schedulingStore all times as UTC; convert on frontend
Cancelled interviews still detected as conflictsFilter by status: 'scheduled' in conflict query

How to Validate

  • Schedule interview — verify creation succeeds
  • Schedule overlapping interview — verify 409 conflict response
  • Cancel interview then reschedule same slot — verify no conflict

Collaborative Document Comments with Realtime Updates

Core Definition

Collaborative document comments allow multiple users to add, reply to, and resolve comments on a shared document in real-time. This requires WebSocket-based updates, optimistic UI, and proper conflict resolution for concurrent operations.

Manoj's Project Context

Manoj would build this using Socket.io for real-time updates (from his ContactsHub experience), MongoDB for comment storage with nested replies, and React with optimistic updates for instant UI feedback.

Code Example

// Comment schema with threading
const commentSchema = {
  documentId: String,
  parentId: String | null,  // null = top-level, string = reply
  author: { id: String, name: String },
  content: String,
  resolved: Boolean,
  position: { start: Number, end: Number },  // text selection range
  createdAt: Date,
  updatedAt: Date,
};

// Socket.io real-time comment sync
io.on('connection', (socket) => {
  socket.on('join-document', (documentId) => {
    socket.join(`doc:${documentId}`);
  });

  socket.on('add-comment', async (data) => {
    const comment = await db.comments.insertOne({
      ...data,
      createdAt: new Date(),
      resolved: false,
    });
    // Broadcast to all users viewing this document
    io.to(`doc:${data.documentId}`).emit('comment-added', {
      ...data,
      id: comment.insertedId,
    });
  });

  socket.on('resolve-comment', async ({ commentId, documentId }) => {
    await db.comments.updateOne(
      { _id: new ObjectId(commentId) },
      { $set: { resolved: true, updatedAt: new Date() } }
    );
    io.to(`doc:${documentId}`).emit('comment-resolved', { commentId });
  });
});

// React: Optimistic comment addition
function useComments(documentId: string) {
  const [comments, setComments] = useState([]);

  function addComment(content: string, position: Range) {
    const optimistic = { id: `temp-${Date.now()}`, content, position, author: currentUser };
    setComments(prev => [...prev, optimistic]); // Instant UI update
    socket.emit('add-comment', { documentId, content, position });
  }

  useEffect(() => {
    socket.on('comment-added', (comment) => {
      setComments(prev => {
        // Replace optimistic entry if it exists
        const withoutTemp = prev.filter(c => !c.id.startsWith('temp-'));
        return [...withoutTemp, comment];
      });
    });
  }, []);

  return { comments, addComment };
}

Manoj's Debugging Narrative

Key design: (1) Comments stored with document position (start/end offsets) for inline highlighting. (2) Socket.io rooms per document — only users viewing the same doc receive updates. (3) Optimistic UI adds the comment instantly with a temp ID, then replaces it with the server ID when the broadcast comes back. (4) Threading via `parentId` — null for top-level, ObjectId for replies. (5) Resolved comments stay visible but grayed out.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Comment positions shift when document is editedUse operational transform or recalculate positions on document change
Optimistic update shows then disappears on errorRoll back optimistic entry; show error toast
Socket reconnection misses commentsFetch latest comments from API on reconnect

How to Validate

  • Add comment — appears instantly for all users viewing document
  • Reply to comment — thread displays correctly
  • Resolve comment — all users see it marked as resolved

Notification Preference Center with Audit History

Core Definition

A notification preference center lets users control which notifications they receive and through which channels. An audit history tracks every preference change for compliance and debugging purposes.

Manoj's Project Context

For the HR Leave Management System, Manoj would build a preference center where employees choose email vs in-app for each notification type (leave updates, policy changes, team schedules), with a full audit trail of preference changes.

Code Example

// Prisma schema
model NotificationPreference {
  id        String   @id @default(uuid())
  userId    String
  category  String   // leave_updates, policy_changes, team_schedule
  email     Boolean  @default(true)
  inApp     Boolean  @default(true)
  digest    String   @default("realtime") // realtime, daily, weekly, off
  updatedAt DateTime @updatedAt
  @@unique([userId, category])
}

model PreferenceAudit {
  id        String   @id @default(uuid())
  userId    String
  category  String
  field     String   // which field changed
  oldValue  String
  newValue  String
  changedAt DateTime @default(now())
  changedBy String   // userId (could be admin override)
}

// API: Update preferences with audit logging
app.put('/api/preferences/:category', async (req, res) => {
  const { category } = req.params;
  const userId = req.user.id;
  const updates = req.body; // { email: false, digest: 'daily' }

  const current = await prisma.notificationPreference.findUnique({
    where: { userId_category: { userId, category } },
  });

  // Create audit entries for each changed field
  const auditEntries = Object.entries(updates)
    .filter(([key, value]) => current?.[key] !== value)
    .map(([key, value]) => ({
      userId,
      category,
      field: key,
      oldValue: String(current?.[key] ?? 'default'),
      newValue: String(value),
      changedBy: req.user.id,
    }));

  await prisma.$transaction([
    prisma.notificationPreference.upsert({
      where: { userId_category: { userId, category } },
      create: { userId, category, ...updates },
      update: updates,
    }),
    ...auditEntries.map(entry => prisma.preferenceAudit.create({ data: entry })),
  ]);

  res.json({ updated: true, changes: auditEntries.length });
});

// API: Get audit history
app.get('/api/preferences/audit', async (req, res) => {
  const history = await prisma.preferenceAudit.findMany({
    where: { userId: req.user.id },
    orderBy: { changedAt: 'desc' },
    take: 50,
  });
  res.json(history);
});

Manoj's Debugging Narrative

Key patterns: (1) Unique constraint on (userId, category) ensures one preference per category per user. (2) Upsert creates default preferences on first access. (3) Audit trail records every field change with old/new values — useful when a user says "I didn't turn off emails" (audit shows who changed it and when). (4) Transaction ensures preference update and audit entries are atomic.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
No default preferences for new usersUse upsert with sensible defaults on first access
Audit table grows unboundedSet retention policy (keep 1 year); archive older entries
Admin overrides not trackedchangedBy field records who made the change (user or admin)

How to Validate

  • Change email preference — verify audit entry created with old/new values
  • View audit history — verify chronological list of all changes
  • New user accesses preferences — verify defaults created via upsert

Role-Based Admin Panel with Policy-Driven Access

Core Definition

A policy-driven admin panel uses declarative access policies (JSON-based rules) instead of hardcoded role checks. Policies define who can access which resources under what conditions, making the system flexible and auditable.

Manoj's Project Context

Building on his RBAC experience from the HR Leave Management System, Manoj would create an admin panel where access policies are stored in the database and evaluated at runtime — no code changes needed to add new roles or modify permissions.

Code Example

// Policy schema — stored in database
interface Policy {
  id: string;
  name: string;
  effect: 'allow' | 'deny';
  roles: string[];      // Which roles this policy applies to
  resources: string[];  // e.g., 'leaves:*', 'employees:read'
  conditions?: {        // Optional runtime conditions
    field: string;
    operator: 'eq' | 'in' | 'ownedBy';
    value: string;
  }[];
}

// Example policies
const policies: Policy[] = [
  {
    id: '1', name: 'Employee self-service',
    effect: 'allow', roles: ['employee'],
    resources: ['leaves:create', 'leaves:read', 'profile:read', 'profile:update'],
    conditions: [{ field: 'userId', operator: 'ownedBy', value: 'requestor' }],
  },
  {
    id: '2', name: 'Manager team access',
    effect: 'allow', roles: ['manager'],
    resources: ['leaves:approve', 'leaves:read', 'reports:team'],
    conditions: [{ field: 'teamId', operator: 'eq', value: 'requestor.teamId' }],
  },
  {
    id: '3', name: 'Admin full access',
    effect: 'allow', roles: ['admin'],
    resources: ['*'],
  },
];

// Policy evaluation engine
function evaluateAccess(user: User, resource: string, context: Record): boolean {
  const matchingPolicies = policies.filter(p =>
    p.roles.includes(user.role) &&
    p.resources.some(r => r === '*' || r === resource || resource.startsWith(r.replace('*', '')))
  );

  // Deny takes precedence
  if (matchingPolicies.some(p => p.effect === 'deny')) return false;

  return matchingPolicies.some(p => {
    if (!p.conditions) return p.effect === 'allow';
    return p.conditions.every(c => evaluateCondition(c, user, context));
  });
}

// Middleware using policy engine
function authorize(resource: string) {
  return (req, res, next) => {
    const allowed = evaluateAccess(req.user, resource, { userId: req.params.id, teamId: req.user.teamId });
    if (!allowed) return res.status(403).json({ error: 'Access denied by policy' });
    next();
  };
}

// Admin API to manage policies (no redeploy needed!)
app.put('/api/admin/policies/:id', authorize('policies:update'), async (req, res) => {
  await db.policies.updateOne({ _id: req.params.id }, { $set: req.body });
  policyCache.invalidate(); // Refresh cached policies
  res.json({ updated: true });
});

Manoj's Debugging Narrative

The key advantage over hardcoded RBAC: when the HR team needed a new "Department Head" role with cross-team leave visibility but no approval rights, it was a database insert (new policy) rather than a code change. Policies are cached in memory with 60-second TTL. The admin panel lets authorized admins create/modify policies via UI, with an audit trail of all policy changes.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Policy conflicts (allow + deny for same resource)Deny always wins; log conflicts for admin review
Policy evaluation performanceCache policies in memory; evaluate locally per request
Broken policy locks out adminsSuper-admin bypass; policy change audit with rollback

How to Validate

  • Create new role via policy — verify access without code change
  • Add deny policy — verify it overrides allow policies
  • Modify policy via admin panel — verify change takes effect within 60s

Resilient Webhook Ingestion Service with Retries

Core Definition

A resilient webhook ingestion service receives external webhooks reliably — handling signature verification, deduplication, persistent queuing, retry with exponential backoff, and dead-letter queuing for permanently failed events.

Manoj's Project Context

Manoj would build this for ContactsHub to reliably ingest contact sync webhooks from external CRM systems. The service must handle burst traffic, never lose an event, and process failed events through a dead-letter queue for manual inspection.

Code Example

// Webhook ingestion with persistent queue and retry
const RETRY_DELAYS = [1000, 5000, 30000, 120000, 600000]; // 1s, 5s, 30s, 2m, 10m

interface WebhookEvent {
  id: string;
  source: string;
  payload: any;
  signature: string;
  receivedAt: Date;
  attempts: number;
  nextRetryAt: Date | null;
  status: 'pending' | 'processing' | 'completed' | 'dead_letter';
  lastError: string | null;
}

// Step 1: Receive and persist immediately
app.post('/webhooks/:source', async (req, res) => {
  // Persist FIRST — never lose an event
  const event = await db.webhookEvents.insertOne({
    id: req.headers['x-event-id'] || crypto.randomUUID(),
    source: req.params.source,
    payload: req.body,
    signature: req.headers['x-signature'],
    receivedAt: new Date(),
    attempts: 0,
    nextRetryAt: new Date(),
    status: 'pending',
    lastError: null,
  });

  res.status(202).json({ received: true, eventId: event.insertedId });
});

// Step 2: Worker processes pending events
async function processWebhookQueue() {
  const events = await db.webhookEvents.find({
    status: 'pending',
    nextRetryAt: { $lte: new Date() },
  }).sort({ receivedAt: 1 }).limit(10).toArray();

  for (const event of events) {
    await db.webhookEvents.updateOne({ _id: event._id }, { $set: { status: 'processing' } });

    try {
      // Verify signature
      if (!verifySignature(event.source, event.payload, event.signature)) {
        throw new Error('Invalid signature');
      }

      // Idempotency check
      const existing = await db.processedEvents.findOne({ eventId: event.id });
      if (existing) { await markCompleted(event._id); continue; }

      // Process based on source
      await processEvent(event.source, event.payload);
      await markCompleted(event._id);
      await db.processedEvents.insertOne({ eventId: event.id, processedAt: new Date() });
    } catch (err) {
      event.attempts++;
      if (event.attempts >= RETRY_DELAYS.length) {
        // Move to dead letter queue
        await db.webhookEvents.updateOne({ _id: event._id }, {
          $set: { status: 'dead_letter', lastError: err.message },
        });
      } else {
        // Schedule retry with exponential backoff
        await db.webhookEvents.updateOne({ _id: event._id }, {
          $set: {
            status: 'pending',
            attempts: event.attempts,
            nextRetryAt: new Date(Date.now() + RETRY_DELAYS[event.attempts]),
            lastError: err.message,
          },
        });
      }
    }
  }
}

// Run worker every 5 seconds
setInterval(processWebhookQueue, 5000);

Manoj's Debugging Narrative

Design principles: (1) Persist before processing — the HTTP handler saves the raw event to MongoDB and responds 202 immediately. Even if the server crashes, the event is never lost. (2) Worker polls for pending events and processes them with retry. (3) Exponential backoff: 1s → 5s → 30s → 2m → 10m. (4) After 5 failed attempts, events move to dead_letter status for manual inspection. (5) Idempotency via processedEvents collection prevents duplicate processing on retries.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Events lost if server crashes before processingPersist to DB before responding; process from persistent queue
Infinite retries waste resourcesCap retries with dead-letter queue for manual inspection
Burst traffic overwhelms workerLimit batch size (10); worker processes at its own pace

How to Validate

  • Send webhook — verify 202 response and event persisted
  • Fail processing — verify retry with increasing delays
  • Exhaust retries — verify event moves to dead_letter

Payments Reconciliation Report Pipeline

Core Definition

A payments reconciliation pipeline compares transaction records from different sources (application database, payment gateway, bank statements) to identify discrepancies — missing transactions, amount mismatches, and duplicate charges.

Manoj's Project Context

For the HR Leave Management System (leave encashment payouts), Manoj would build a reconciliation pipeline that compares the application's payout records against Stripe's transaction log, generating a report of any discrepancies for the finance team.

Code Example

// Reconciliation pipeline
interface ReconciliationResult {
  matched: { appId: string; stripeId: string; amount: number }[];
  missingInStripe: { appId: string; amount: number }[];
  missingInApp: { stripeId: string; amount: number }[];
  amountMismatch: { appId: string; stripeId: string; appAmount: number; stripeAmount: number }[];
}

async function reconcilePayments(startDate: Date, endDate: Date): Promise {
  // Fetch from both sources
  const [appPayments, stripePayments] = await Promise.all([
    prisma.payout.findMany({
      where: { createdAt: { gte: startDate, lte: endDate }, status: 'completed' },
    }),
    fetchStripePayments(startDate, endDate),
  ]);

  const result: ReconciliationResult = {
    matched: [], missingInStripe: [], missingInApp: [], amountMismatch: [],
  };

  // Index Stripe payments by reference ID for O(1) lookup
  const stripeMap = new Map(stripePayments.map(p => [p.metadata.appPayoutId, p]));
  const matchedStripeIds = new Set();

  // Compare app records against Stripe
  for (const appPay of appPayments) {
    const stripePay = stripeMap.get(appPay.id);

    if (!stripePay) {
      result.missingInStripe.push({ appId: appPay.id, amount: appPay.amount });
    } else if (Math.abs(appPay.amount - stripePay.amount / 100) > 0.01) {
      result.amountMismatch.push({
        appId: appPay.id, stripeId: stripePay.id,
        appAmount: appPay.amount, stripeAmount: stripePay.amount / 100,
      });
      matchedStripeIds.add(stripePay.id);
    } else {
      result.matched.push({ appId: appPay.id, stripeId: stripePay.id, amount: appPay.amount });
      matchedStripeIds.add(stripePay.id);
    }
  }

  // Find Stripe payments with no app record
  for (const stripePay of stripePayments) {
    if (!matchedStripeIds.has(stripePay.id)) {
      result.missingInApp.push({ stripeId: stripePay.id, amount: stripePay.amount / 100 });
    }
  }

  return result;
}

// Generate report endpoint
app.get('/api/admin/reconciliation', authorize('reports:financial'), async (req, res) => {
  const { startDate, endDate } = req.query;
  const result = await reconcilePayments(new Date(startDate), new Date(endDate));
  res.json({
    summary: {
      total: result.matched.length + result.missingInStripe.length + result.missingInApp.length,
      matched: result.matched.length,
      discrepancies: result.missingInStripe.length + result.missingInApp.length + result.amountMismatch.length,
    },
    ...result,
  });
});

Manoj's Debugging Narrative

Key patterns: (1) Fetch from both sources in parallel with `Promise.all`. (2) Index one source into a Map for O(1) lookups — avoids O(n²) comparison. (3) Three types of discrepancies: missing in either source, and amount mismatch (using 0.01 tolerance for floating point). (4) Stripe amounts are in cents; app amounts in rupees — always normalize before comparing. (5) Reports are generated on-demand for a date range, not stored, keeping them always fresh.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Floating point comparison errorsUse epsilon tolerance (0.01) for amount matching
Currency unit mismatch (cents vs dollars)Normalize all amounts to same unit before comparison
Large date ranges cause slow queriesPaginate or stream results; add database indexes on date

How to Validate

  • Seed test data with known discrepancies — verify report catches all
  • Test with matching records — verify matched count is correct
  • Test with large dataset — verify performance is acceptable

Feature Toggle Platform with Staged Rollout

Core Definition

A feature toggle platform provides a centralized system for managing feature flags with staged rollout capabilities — canary (1%), beta (10%), GA (100%) — with real-time toggle control, user segmentation, and rollout metrics.

Manoj's Project Context

Building on his feature flag experience from ContactsHub, Manoj would create a self-hosted toggle platform that serves flags via API with sub-10ms latency, supports percentage-based staged rollouts, and provides a dashboard showing rollout health metrics.

Code Example

// Feature toggle with staged rollout
interface Toggle {
  name: string;
  description: string;
  enabled: boolean;
  stages: {
    name: string;       // canary, beta, ga
    percent: number;    // 1, 10, 100
    active: boolean;
  }[];
  currentStage: string;
  targeting: {
    allowUserIds?: string[];  // Explicit allow-list
    allowRoles?: string[];    // Role-based targeting
  };
  metrics: {
    impressions: number;
    errors: number;
    lastError?: string;
  };
}

class TogglePlatform {
  private toggles = new Map();
  private cache: Toggle[] = [];

  async evaluate(toggleName: string, user: { id: string; role: string }): Promise {
    const toggle = this.toggles.get(toggleName);
    if (!toggle || !toggle.enabled) return false;

    // Explicit allow-list check
    if (toggle.targeting.allowUserIds?.includes(user.id)) return true;
    if (toggle.targeting.allowRoles?.some(r => r === user.role)) return true;

    // Staged rollout: consistent hashing
    const currentStage = toggle.stages.find(s => s.name === toggle.currentStage);
    if (!currentStage?.active) return false;

    const bucket = this.hashToBucket(toggleName, user.id);
    const enabled = bucket < currentStage.percent;

    // Track metrics
    toggle.metrics.impressions++;
    return enabled;
  }

  // Advance to next stage
  async advanceStage(toggleName: string): Promise {
    const toggle = this.toggles.get(toggleName)!;
    const currentIdx = toggle.stages.findIndex(s => s.name === toggle.currentStage);
    const nextStage = toggle.stages[currentIdx + 1];

    if (!nextStage) throw new Error('Already at final stage');

    // Safety check: error rate below threshold
    const errorRate = toggle.metrics.errors / Math.max(toggle.metrics.impressions, 1);
    if (errorRate > 0.05) {
      throw new Error(`Error rate ${(errorRate*100).toFixed(1)}% exceeds 5% threshold. Fix errors before advancing.`);
    }

    toggle.currentStage = nextStage.name;
    nextStage.active = true;
    await this.persist(toggle);
  }

  // Instant kill-switch
  async disable(toggleName: string): Promise {
    const toggle = this.toggles.get(toggleName)!;
    toggle.enabled = false;
    await this.persist(toggle);
  }

  private hashToBucket(toggleName: string, userId: string): number {
    // Consistent hash: same user always gets same bucket
    let hash = 0;
    const str = `${toggleName}:${userId}`;
    for (let i = 0; i < str.length; i++) {
      hash = ((hash << 5) - hash + str.charCodeAt(i)) | 0;
    }
    return Math.abs(hash) % 100;
  }
}

// API endpoints
app.get('/api/toggles/evaluate', async (req, res) => {
  const flags = {};
  for (const [name] of platform.toggles) {
    flags[name] = await platform.evaluate(name, req.user);
  }
  res.json(flags);
});

app.post('/api/admin/toggles/:name/advance', authorize('toggles:manage'), async (req, res) => {
  await platform.advanceStage(req.params.name);
  res.json({ advanced: true });
});

Manoj's Debugging Narrative

Key design: (1) Three predefined stages: canary (1% of users), beta (10%), GA (100%). (2) Stage advancement requires error rate below 5% — prevents rolling out broken features. (3) Consistent hashing ensures the same user always sees the same flag state (no flickering). (4) Kill-switch instantly disables a toggle for all users. (5) Metrics track impressions and errors per toggle for rollout health monitoring. The dashboard shows error rate per stage, enabling data-driven rollout decisions.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Advancing despite high error rateEnforce error rate threshold before stage advancement
Inconsistent flag evaluation across requestsConsistent hashing on userId ensures deterministic results
Slow evaluation blocks requestsIn-memory cache with background refresh; sub-10ms evaluation

How to Validate

  • Create toggle at canary (1%) — verify ~1% of users see feature
  • Advance to beta — verify ~10% now see feature
  • Inject errors — verify advancement blocked above 5% error rate
  • Kill-switch — verify feature instantly disabled for all users
